coredata.dc:title: Physicochemical and in vitro characterization of Agarose based nanocarriers incorporated with Graphene Quantum Dots/ α-Fe2O3 for targeted drug delivery of Quercetin to liver cancer treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Najafi, Mohammad
Khoddam, Zahra
Masnavi, Mobina
Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Abdouss, Majid
coredata.dc:description: The forefront hydrogel nanocomposite, resulting from the integration of Agarose (Aga), Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), and α-Fe2O3, offers a novel approach to pH-responsive drug delivery, specifically tailored for the controlled release of Quercetin (QC), a potent anti-cancer agent. The incorporation of the surfactant Span 80 enhances the unique properties conferred by the integration of α-Fe2O3 into the Aga-modified GQDs hydrogel, resulting in the production of stable nanocarriers that improve QC retention. Thorough analyses utilizing Fourier-transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray Diffraction (XRD) shed light on the characteristics of the nanocarriers. The presence of nanoparticles within the nanocarriers was confirmed by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), revealing a spherical morphology with an average size of approximately 279.04 nm and a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.24. Measurements of the zeta potential indicated a positive surface charge of about 52.8 mV. In the Aga/GQDs configuration, loading and encapsulation efficiencies reached 35 % and 67 %, respectively. With the inclusion of α-Fe2O3, improvements were observed, with loading and encapsulation efficiencies reaching 47 % and 86.25 %, respectively, surpassing previous values. Cellular tests conducted on the HepG2 cell line demonstrated the heightened anticancer efficacy of QC-loaded nanoparticles while assessing potential adverse effects. These results underscore the exciting potential of the nanocarrier as a promising strategy for cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iron oxide
Nanocarrier
Graphene quantum dots
Quercetin
Drug delivery
Cancer treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparing Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDs nanocarrier and loading of quercetin
Evaluation of drug loading and entrapment efficiency
Drug release study in laboratory conditions
Providing cell cultures and assessing cytotoxicity in laboratory setting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A variety of samples had their FT-IR spectra taken, including: a) Aga, b) GQDs c) α-Fe2O3, d) Aga/GQDS, e) Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3 nanocomposite, f) Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3@QC nanocarrier.
XRD spectra of: a) Aga, b) GQDs, c) α-Fe2O3, d) Aga/GQDS, e) Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3 nanocomposite, and f) Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3@QC nanocarrier.
The FE-SEM images that represent the Aga/GQDS α-Fe2O3 @QC nanocarriers are shown. The photos to the left and right show scale bars that represent 200 and 500 nm, respectively.

The graph on the left illustrates the particle size distribution obtained from Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) experiments. Simultaneously, on the right side, the intensity distribution of zeta potential unveils distinctive features of the Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3@QC nanocarriers.
Quercetin release profile for Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDS hydrogel and for nanocarriers encapsulating quercetin-loaded Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDS hydrogel nanocomposite and Aga/α-Fe2O3 without GQDS at pH 5.4 and 7.4.

Displays the results of the MTT test, which was conducted over the course of 24 h for both HepG2 and L929 cells. After the studies were run three times, the data is shown as mean values with corresponding standard errors of the mean (SEM). A thorough statistical analysis was performed to identify any significant differences between the tested samples and the control group. More specifically, the ** symbol denotes a significant level below 0.01 (p < 0.01), and the *** symbol denotes a level below 0.001 (p < 0.001).
Proposed synthesis method for drug-loaded Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDs nanocomposite.
The efficiency of loading and encapsulation for Aga/GQDs hydrogel without α-Fe2O3 and Aga/GQDs hydrogel with α-Fe2O3.

head.ce:title: Physicochemical and in vitro characterization of Agarose based nanocarriers incorporated with Graphene Quantum Dots/ α-Fe2O3 for targeted drug delivery of Quercetin to liver cancer treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Amirkabir University of Technology, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Amirkabir University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, GC, 1983963113, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, GC, 1983963113, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The forefront hydrogel nanocomposite, resulting from the integration of Agarose (Aga), Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), and α-Fe2O3, offers a novel approach to pH-responsive drug delivery, specifically tailored for the controlled release of Quercetin (QC), a potent anti-cancer agent. The incorporation of the surfactant Span 80 enhances the unique properties conferred by the integration of α-Fe2O3 into the Aga-modified GQDs hydrogel, resulting in the production of stable nanocarriers that improve QC retention. Thorough analyses utilizing Fourier-transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray Diffraction (XRD) shed light on the characteristics of the nanocarriers. The presence of nanoparticles within the nanocarriers was confirmed by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), revealing a spherical morphology with an average size of approximately 279.04 nm and a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.24. Measurements of the zeta potential indicated a positive surface charge of about 52.8 mV. In the Aga/GQDs configuration, loading and encapsulation efficiencies reached 35 % and 67 %, respectively. With the inclusion of α-Fe2O3, improvements were observed, with loading and encapsulation efficiencies reaching 47 % and 86.25 %, respectively, surpassing previous values. Cellular tests conducted on the HepG2 cell line demonstrated the heightened anticancer efficacy of QC-loaded nanoparticles while assessing potential adverse effects. These results underscore the exciting potential of the nanocarrier as a promising strategy for cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the present day, the mention of cancer instills deep-seated fear and uncertainty in individuals, often casting a shadow of despair. Cancer, which manifests in various forms, stems from the abnormal proliferation of cells within the human body [1]. Our body comprises countless cells, each meticulously guided by its genetic instructions. Occasionally, due to vague genetic cues or anomalies, a cell deviates from its regular course, setting the stage for the emergence of abnormal cells that can traverse the bloodstream or immune system, potentially culminating in malignant tumors or cancer [2]. Chemotherapy, a widely embraced approach, centers on impeding or halting the rapid proliferation of cancer cells but often incurs collateral damage to healthy cells, leading to an array of side effects [3]. Another method, radiotherapy, inflicts damage on cancerous cells but lacks the precision to spare neighboring healthy ones, potentially giving rise to nutritional complications. The quest for a cancer treatment strategy that mitigates side effects, reduces financial burdens, and minimizes disruption to everyday life has long posed a formidable medical challenge. Consequently, scientists have turned to innovative methods rooted in nanoscience. Nanotechnology is now gaining substantial recognition across diverse medical fields, particularly in cancer treatment. This cutting-edge science has revolutionized both disease diagnosis and treatment by harnessing the potential of nanoparticles and pioneering novel techniques for targeted drug delivery [4,5].

The effective delivery of specific cytotoxic agents to tumor sites while minimizing unforeseen adverse side effects remains a significant challenge in the field of chemotherapy. Drug delivery systems (DDS) have emerged as a vital avenue for optimizing the pharmacokinetics and distribution of drugs, thereby enhancing the therapeutic potential of conventional chemotherapeutics. Controlled-release nanodrug delivery systems have gained substantial recognition due to their ability to overcome pharmacological limitations and confer notable advantages over traditional dosing methods, especially in the context of cancer treatment. The use of polymeric nanoparticle (NP) systems for drug delivery is one particularly promising strategy. These systems provide both targeted distribution and prolonged release, greatly enhancing the therapeutic efficacy of pharmaceuticals [6].

Utilizing green nanomaterials for drug delivery involves employing environmentally friendly and sustainable substances derived from natural sources. This approach prioritizes the use of biocompatible and biodegradable substances, including plant extracts or biopolymers, to fabricate nanocarriers for drug transport applications [7,8]. By harnessing the unique properties of these environmentally sustainable nanomaterials, such as their inherent biocompatibility and minimal toxicity, researchers aim to develop drug delivery platforms that are both safe and effective, while also reducing environmental impact and fostering sustainability. As an example, in a study conducted by Firat Baran et al. [9]. examine the green fabrication of silver nanoparticles utilizing Allium cepa peel extract and their characteristics. The synthesized nanoparticles demonstrate antimicrobial, antioxidant, and anticholinesterase activities. Noteworthy observations involve nanoparticle characterization and their impact on pathogens and enzymes. This environmentally conscious synthesis method presents promising applications across multiple domains.

Because of their stability, convenience of use, and capacity to lower medication toxicity, micro/nano-emulsions are becoming more and more popular for the delivery of drugs. They have potential applications in various delivery methods, including oral, topical, ocular, and injections [10]. In cancer treatment, their small particle size can enhance drug release and circulation time. These emulsions are less painful on injection compared to other methods. However, rigorous testing, including stability, compatibility, and safety assessments, is necessary before their use, especially for injections. They offer promise for delivering hydrophobic drugs, such as anticancer medications, intravenously. When considering parenteral administration, rigorous testing is essential to ensure safety and suitability. Examining droplet size, optical properties, osmolarity, pH, viscosity and physical and chemical stability are all included in this. Both in vitro erythrocyte toxicity and compatibility with intravenous infusions need to be assessed. Additionally, potential injection-related pain should be investigated, and in vivo anesthetic activity may need assessment. Nanocarriere have great solubilization potential, which makes them promising as vehicles for intravenous drug delivery and have been formulated and tested for hemodynamic responses [11–13]. As an example, in a study conducted by Eftekhari et al. [14]. Offer an extensive exploration of nanomaterials in cancer therapy, emphasizing their capacity to enhance drug delivery by improving stability, biocompatibility, and cellular uptake. Nanocarriers facilitate precise drug delivery to target organs, enhancing pharmacokinetics while minimizing adverse effects on non-target tissues. The authors review diverse nanovectors, such as liposomes, dendrimers, nanoparticles, and quantum dots, analyzing their mechanisms of action and pharmacokinetics in multimodal cancer treatment and diagnosis.

Quercetin (often abbreviated as QC) is a naturally occurring flavonoid compound that can be found abundantly in various vegetables, fruits, seeds, and leaves. Because of its pro-apoptotic and anticancer characteristics, it has attracted a lot of attention [15]. It is well established that QC not only makes chemotherapy drugs more toxic, but also amplifies their antitumor effects. As a result, it is commonly advised as an antioxidant to prevent cancer. Numerous studies have demonstrated the critical function that QC plays in suppressing cancer cells in a variety of organ systems, including as the lung, breast, ovary, colon, and prostate. Because of its strong antiproliferative activity, it can treat a variety of cancer types, making them more amenable to chemotherapy drugs and improving the effectiveness of treatment. Beyond its anticancer potential, QC has demonstrated therapeutic benefits, including antibacterial, antidiabetic, anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and cardiovascular health-enhancing properties. QC's role isn't limited to its well-documented effects on cancer. It also influences pro-inflammatory cytokines, affecting various physiological processes. To address challenges associated with QC, such as issues with oxidation, isomerization, decomposition, and limited permeability, scientists have found value in using degradable polymer carriers. This approach enhances QC's stability and provides controlled and sustained drug delivery to specific body tissues [16,17].

Hydrogels, versatile polymeric materials, can be fabricated from natural or synthetic sources, offering biocompatibility, biodegradability, and non-toxicity. This research employs three specific compounds: Agarose (Aga), Iron oxide(α-Fe2O3) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

Agarose (Aga) stands out as a linear polysaccharide known for its distinctive role in forming hydrogels for drug entrapment. Aga-based hydrogels are renowned for their outstanding stability and biocompatibility. Furthermore, the integration of Aga into the hydrogel structure imparts a degree of sensitivity to pH variations. To create an Aga gel, a series of heating and cooling steps are employed. Aga's remarkable ability to create thermally reversible gels has found diverse applications, spanning DNA electrophoresis, tissue engineering, the food industry, and drug delivery, among others [18].

Iron oxide nanoparticles possess several advantageous attributes, including biocompatibility, magnetic responsiveness, electrochemical stability, safety, cost-effectiveness, and abundance. These characteristics have established them as a prominent category of inorganic nanoparticles with diverse applications in areas like tumor therapy, vaccine delivery platforms, clinical settings, and drug delivery [19]. Within the spectrum of iron oxide variants (α, β, γ-Fe2O3), α-Fe2O3 emerges as the most environmentally friendly and structurally stable material. Researchers have developed various methods to synthesize these nanoparticles, allowing for precise control of their size and achieving minimal size variations [20]. In this particular investigation, α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles were synthesized using the hydrothermal method.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials known for their potential in drug delivery. Their distinctive attributes, such as biocompatibility, a large surface area, and facile modification with targeting ligands, render them highly valuable in the field of drug delivery. GQDs can protect drugs, enable precise delivery, and provide imaging functions, offering the prospect of reducing side effects by targeting specific cells or tissues. Researchers are actively engaged in exploring the potential of GQDs in drug delivery systems to enhance therapeutic efficacy while mitigating adverse reactions [21,22].

The utilization of these nanocarriers offers several advantages in the realm of cancer treatment. They exhibit improved efficiency in drug delivery, heightened cellular uptake, sustained release patterns, targeted localization to tumor cells, multifaceted functionalities, and favorable biocompatibility. These attributes collectively propel forward the frontier of biomedical applications for cancer therapy.

In our research, we set out to explore the impact of Quercetin -loaded nanocarriers on inducing programmed cell death (apoptosis) in cancer cells, with a particular focus on HepG2 liver cancer cells. To increase the drug delivery efficiency, we incorporated GQDs into Aga/α-Fe2O3 nanocomposites. Our approach was inspired by a prior study that employed a double emulsification technique (water in oil in water) to produce polysaccharide nanocarriers. This method is renowned for its capacity to produce stable and uniformly-sized nanocarriers, thanks to the use of surfactants. Despite Quercetin being a hydrophobic substance, our incorporation process ensured that it remained within the hydrogel phase rather than migrating to the oil phase. We conducted a comprehensive characterization of these nanocarriers using various analytical techniques, including Fourier-transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), and Zeta potential assays. These analyses allowed us to determine crucial parameters such as drug loading efficiency and release profiles. Subsequently, we delved into a rigorous evaluation of their anticancer potential. MTT assays were meticulously conducted on both HepG2 liver cancer cells and the L929 cell line. Our goal was to investigate their effectiveness in inducing apoptosis and their suitability as a potential treatment option for liver cancer.

The Fe(NO3)3.9H2O was acquired from the Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology. Aga, span 80, acetic acid (99.5 %), absolute ethanol, and were purchased from Merck (Germany). QGDs were prepared as powder. Penicillin-Streptomycin was obtained from the Beijing Institute of Science and Technology, and PBS (phosphate buffer saline, 0.1 M, pH 7.4) was obtained from the Iranian Biological Resource Center. Quercetin, dialysis Tubing Cellulose Membrane(33 mm*21 mm), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) and MTT powder were acquired from Sigma Aldrich Company.

In the synthesis of α-Fe2O3, a homogeneous solution was created by combining 1.8 gr of Fe(NO3)3.9H2O dispersed in 20 mL of distilled water. The pH level was adjusted to approximately 9 by introducing NH3.H2O into the solution with continuous stirring. After stirring for a period of 20 min, the solution was moved into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. This sealed container underwent a heating process and was held at 120 °C for 10 h in an oven, subsequently undergoing natural cooling to attain room temperature [23]. Subsequent to this, the resultant materials experienced consecutive rinses using distilled water and absolute ethanol, concluding with a drying stage at 80 °C for 12 h [24].

10 gr of citric acid undergoes a transformation through heating at 200 °C for a duration of 5 min. Following this process, the resulting yellow liquid undergoes a color shift to orange after 30 min, signaling the emergence of GQDs [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparing Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDs nanocarrier and loading of quercetin
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, on a thermal stirrer at room temperature, 0.5 gr of Aga was added to 25 mL of 2 % acetic acid and thoroughly dissolved, yielding a homogenous gel-like solution of 2 % Aga. The hydrogel formed in the previous stage is mixed with 25 mg of α-Fe2O3 powder until it is completely dissolved. Next, it is placed in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. Then add the amount of 25 mg of GQDs previously prepared to the above solution so that it is completely homogenized on the heater stirrer. The following stage involves loading the QC drug so that the final hydrogel has a drug concentration of 5 μg/ml. The hydrogel is then placed on a heating stirrer for 30 min, and the result is a one-handed Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDs hydrogel containing QC drug. The one-handed Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDs hydrogel containing quercetin is being vigorously swirled with a magnet on the stirrer. In the following phase, we use a sampler to add 2 % (v/v) surfactant SPAN 80 drop by drop until the drug-carrying nanocarrier forms a spherical shape [26,27]. Drop by drop, add 25 mL of distilled water for washing the mixture after 30 min. After 15 min, take the solution out of the stirrer and give the layers 20 min to separate. The drug-containing nanocarrier is then separated from the aqueous phase using a centrifuge set to 4500 rpm for 8 min. We grind each sample into various sizes using a freeze dryer. We freeze dry it after putting it in the fridge at −20 °C until it freezes entirely (Scheme 1 ) [23,25,28].

The examination of the surface characteristics of the synthesized nanocomposite Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDs was conducted through Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM: TESCAN, MIRA3, Czech), The analysis was performed using an accelerating voltage of 15 kV at a suitable magnification. Investigation the average size distribution of nanoemulsions and their surface charge, referred to as zeta potential, were evaluated using dynamic light scattering (DLS) analyzer Zetasizer Nano (Malvern, UK). DLS measures the intensity of light scattered by particles in a sample, providing valuable insights into the distribution of their sizes. An important tool for analyzing the properties of the nanocomposites was Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), revealing intricate details about their functional groups and chemical composition. FTIR analysis using a PerkinElmer Spectrum One spectrophotometer that Equipped with software version 10.03.06, it identifies specific absorption bands associated with different chemical bonds and helps to identify functional groups in nanocomposites. Investigating the crystal structure of nanocomposites through by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, conducted using PAN alytical xPert pro diffractometer and investigated the nanocarrier crystallinity in the 2θ range of 10–80°. XRD analysis involves X-ray scattering by a sample and provides information about its crystal lattice structure and phase composition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of drug loading and entrapment efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
After precisely measuring, 1 mg of the nanocomposite Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDs in 1 mL of phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) and 1 mL of ethyl acetate was added. Then the ethyl acetate phase was separated by agitating the mixture. Through the isolation of the ethyl acetate phase, the liberation of the medication can be achieved [17,25]. There is a clear relationship between the optical density and the amount of free drug in this phase, which was established by measuring the amount of free QC with a UV–Visible spectrophotometer set to 370 nm. Equations (1) and (2) were utilized to compute the percentage of QC entrapment efficiency (EE) and loading efficiency (LE) [29,30].
(1)
E n t r a p m e n t
e f f i c i e n c y
( % )
=
[
(
total
amount
of
QC
)
−
(
free
amount
of
QC
)
]
(
total
amount
of
QC
)
× 100
(2)
L o a d i n g
e f f i c i e n c y
( % )
=

(

amount
of
QC
)
−
(
free
amount
of
QC
)
]
(
total
amount
of
nanoparticles
)
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug release study in laboratory conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
The dialysis method was utilized to evaluate the release of quenching (QC) from the Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDs nanocomposite in PBS media at pH values of 5.4 and 7.4, which correspond to the pH of healthy and malignant tissues, respectively [31]. Subsequently, 1 mL of the nanocarrier was immersed in 15 mL of PBS and enclosed within a dialysis bag at 37 °C (Molecular weight cutoff 12 KDa). At prearranged intervals of time (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h), 300 μl of every sample were extracted and substituted with an equivalent amount of fresh medium (fresh buffer) [32,33]. A spectrophotometer measured at 370 nm was used to measure the amount of drugs released, and the (3) formula was employed to approximate the quantity of QC released
(3)
Q C
r e l e a s e d
( % )
=
(
QC
released
)
(
QC
loaded
)
× 100

A key factor in interpreting the release research is equation (3). Loaded QC describes the amount of drug that is initially added to the nanoparticles. Conversely, Released QC refers to the drug content measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer in the samples that were collected [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Providing cell cultures and assessing cytotoxicity in laboratory setting
ce:section.ce:para:
The MTT assay was performed in order to investigate cytotoxicity on normal cells (L929) and cancer cells (hep G2). For a 48-h period, cells were cultivated and attached to 96-well plates. The control cells were then incubated for 72 h. Following incubation for 4 h, 5 mg/mL MTT (50 μL) was added to each well of the basic medium (DMEM containing 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotic penicillin/streptomycin), along with the free QC and the synthesized nanocarrier (Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDs@QC). Formazan crystals were then dissolved in DMSO. Using an ELISA reader set at 570 nm, the optical density of the dissolved formazan crystals was determined. Additionally, normal cells have undergone the same procedures [35,36]. Then the percentage of cells viability was calculated using equation (4).
(4)
c e l l
v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
[ Absorbance ]
samples
[ Absorbance ]
control
sample
× 100

Through analysis of the detected peaks suggesting interactions between Agarose (Aga), Graphene quantum dots (GQD), Iron oxide (α-Fe2O3), and quercetin (QC) as the model drug, FTIR was used to establish the inclusion of all components in the nanocomposite. In the FTIR spectrum of Aga (Fig. 1 a), FTIR analysis of Aga revealed characteristic peaks at approximately 3300 cm⁻1, indicating O–H stretching vibrations related to the presence of hydroxyl (OH) groups, which are abundant in polysaccharides like Aga. A peak at approximately 2920 cm⁻1 corresponded to C–H stretching vibrations within the Aga structure. The peak at approximately 1600 cm⁻1 is associated with the bending vibrations of O–H groups, signifying the presence of hydrogen bonds within the Aga matrix. Another peak at about 1050 cm⁻1 suggested the existence of C–O–C and C–O–H stretching vibrations inherent to the polysaccharide structure [33,37]. Regarding the spectrum of GQDs(Fig. 1b), The FTIR spectrum of graphene quantum dots revealed peaks around 3400 cm⁻1, indicating the presence of O–H stretching vibrations attributed to surface hydroxyl groups. A peak at approximately 2920 cm⁻1 is associated with C–H stretching vibrations in the carbonaceous structure. The peak at approximately 1600 cm⁻1 signified CO stretching vibrations, pointing to the presence of carbonyl (CO) functional groups on the GQD surface. Peaks at approximately 1100 cm⁻1 and 1050 cm⁻1 are attributed to C–O stretching vibrations, further confirming the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups [38]. The spectrum of α-Fe2O3 (Fig. 1c), FTIR analysis of iron oxide (α-Fe2O3) displayed prominent peaks at around 550 cm⁻1, indicating the presence of Fe–O stretching vibrations, which are characteristic of iron oxide compounds. A peak in the region of 1630–1670 cm⁻1 corresponds to O–H bending vibrations and may arise from absorbed water molecules or hydroxyl groups on the iron oxide surface [39]. In the FTIR spectrum of Aga/GQDS(Fig. 1d), Peaks at approximately 3400 cm⁻1 were indicative of O–H stretching vibrations related to surface hydroxyl groups. A peak at approximately 2920 cm⁻1 corresponds to C–H stretching vibrations in the carbonaceous structure. A peak around 1600 cm⁻1 may indicate CO stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of carbonyl (CO) functional groups. Peaks at roughly 1100 cm⁻1 and 1050 cm⁻1 indicated C–O stretching vibrations, affirming the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups. The FTIR spectrum of Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3 (Fig. 1e) displayed peaks at around 550 cm⁻1, indicating the presence of Fe–O stretching vibrations, characteristic of iron oxide compounds. Peaks associated with Aga/GQDS, such as those at 3400 cm⁻1 and 1600 cm⁻1, likely remain in the spectrum, indicating the continued presence of Aga/GQDS within the composite. The FTIR spectrum of Aga/GQDS α-Fe2O3@QC may show additional peaks associated with quercetin (QC), a flavonoid compound. These peaks could include those related to O–H stretching vibrations, C–H stretching vibrations, and specific functional groups present in QC, depending on the QC loading and interactions within the composite [17,27].

The interpretation of X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis provides valuable insights into the structural properties of the components and composite materials studied. The X-ray diffractogram of Aga (Fig. 2 a) displayed clear peaks at specific 2θ angles, such as 19.2° and 24.0°, providing evidence for the crystalline nature of Aga. This pattern highlights the structured and organized arrangement of molecules in Aga, suggesting potential implications for its applications [40,41]. The XRD analysis of GQDs (Fig. 2b) reveals distinct peaks at 2θ angles of 38.0° and 44.94°, corresponding to the hexagonal lattice structure. This characteristic pattern confirms the crystalline nature of GQDs and provides valuable information about their structural arrangement [42]. The XRD pattern for α-Fe2O3 (Fig. 2c) reveals distinct and well-defined peaks at 2θ angles of 33.24°, 35.84°, 45.59°, 54.14°, 62.39°, and 63.49°. These well-defined peaks correspond to the crystallographic characteristics of hematite, which is in line with findings published by Wang et al. [24]. The XRD examination of the Aga/GQDS composite (Fig. 2d) demonstrates well-defined peaks at particular 2θ angles, such as 19.2° and 24.0°, confirming the crystalline nature of Aga. The presence of graphene quantum dots introduces unique characteristics, suggesting a successful blend of Aga and graphene quantum dots with potential synergistic effects. The XRD analysis of the Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3 composite (Fig. 2e) demonstrates clear peaks at specific 2θ angles, such as 19.2° and 24.0°, confirming the crystalline nature Aga. This pattern also reflects characteristic peaks at 33.21° and 35.85°, indicative of the crystal structures of graphene GQDS and Fe (III) oxide within the composite. There was a discernible increase in the intensity of the characteristic peak at 2θ = 33.21° after adding QC to the Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3 nanoemulsion. This significant increase suggests that QC has been successfully entrapped within the porous structure of α-Fe2O3. As depicted in Fig. 2f, this finding underscores the successful integration of QC into the composite, emphasizing its potential implications for drug delivery applications [17,43].

The FESEM results for Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3@QC offer valuable insights into the nanocomposite's surface morphology and structure, particularly relevant for drug delivery applications. Observing Fig. 3 , the micrographs depict a well-dispersed and quasi-spherical arrangement of particles within the range of ∼ 100–250 nm. Moreover, the deliberate addition of the surfactant Span 80 was crucial for the formation of niosomal emulsions organized in core-shell clusters. The well-dispersed GQDS and α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles within the Agar matrix contribute to the formation of a stable and homogeneous nanocomposite structure. Upon closer inspection at higher magnification, the micrographs reveal a porous and interconnected network, showcasing potential advantages for efficient drug loading and release. Additionally, the smooth surface observed in the micrographs implies enhanced biocompatibility, an essential feature for the nanocomposite's potential use in biomedical contexts [19,44].

The analysis of Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and zeta potential for Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3@QC nanocomposite provide crucial insights into its characteristics for drug delivery applications. With an average particle size of around 279.04 nm (Fig. 4 , left), falling within the nanoscale range, which is advantageous for effective drug delivery. The low Polydispersity Index (PDI) of 0.24 indicates a uniform particle size distribution, ensuring stability in the colloidal system [36,45].

The zeta potential analysis (Fig. 4, right), showcasing a surface charge of 52.8 mV, points towards a charged nanocomposite, contributing to electrostatic repulsion between particles and preventing aggregation. The nonionic surfactant Span 80 is added to the formulation to facilitate the development of niosomal nanoemulsions. This increases the nanoemulsions' resistance to particle aggregation, enabling longer storage times and ensuring the nanocarrier system operates as efficiently as feasible. In the realm of drug delivery, this charged surface could influence cellular interactions, possibly enhancing cellular uptake and targeted delivery. In essence, the nanocomposite demonstrates favorable characteristics, such as nanoscale size, uniform distribution, and a charged surface, all of which contribute to its potential as an effective candidate for drug delivery in therapeutic applications [25,46].

As previously mentioned, the limited solubility of QC results in a very short biological half-life, which significantly reduces its therapeutic effectiveness. The goal of current research is to improve the efficiency of QC's loading and encapsulation within nanocarriers in order to address this problem. To assess the impact of adding inorganic nanoparticles to the polymeric matrix, we calculated the efficiency of loading and encapsulation for Aga/GQDs hydrogel and Aga/GQDs hydrogel with α-Fe2O3 in this context. For Aga/GQDs alone, our calculations yielded loading and encapsulation efficiencies of 35 % and 67 %, respectively (detailed in Table 1 ). However, with the inclusion of α-Fe2O3, we observed an improvement, resulting in loading and encapsulation efficiencies of 47 % and 86.25 %, respectively. This enhancement in loading and encapsulation efficiencies when introducing inorganic nanoparticles can be attributed to robust interactions among various components of the nanocomposite. The O–H groups of Aga, the CO groups of GQDs, and the α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles are involved in these interactions [47,48]. In combination, these robust interactions create a network of connections that is very conducive for drug loading [27,49]. Moreover, α-Fe2O3 's remarkably extensive surface area is essential for increasing QC loading and encapsulation. This large surface area increases the amount of space available for interactions between the components of the nanocomposite and QC, which enhances the effectiveness of drugs inclusion even more.

To assess drug release profiles from the Aga/α-Fe2O3/GQDs nanocarrier and investigate the impact of GQDs within the nanocomposite hydrogel, we conducted experiments utilizing the dialysis bag method. This study involved two distinct buffer solutions with different pH levels: one simulating the acidic environment of cancerous tissues (pH 4.5) and the other representing the physiological pH of 7.4. These experiments were conducted under controlled conditions at 37 °C over a 96-h duration. The results, as illustrated in Fig. 5 , reveal an intriguing pattern. In the acidic environment resembling cancerous tissues (pH 4.5), quercetin's release exhibited rates of 33 %, 49 %, 66 %, 87 %, and 96 % at 12, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h, respectively. In contrast, within the physiological environment simulating normal bodily conditions (pH 7.4), the drug exhibited considerably lower release rates, with 9 %, 17 %, 30 %, 42 %, and 56 % release over the corresponding time intervals. These findings underscore the sustained and protracted release capabilities of the nanocarrier, particularly evident over the 96-h duration. Moreover, the overarching trend of the release profiles aligns with the pH-responsive nature of this drug delivery system, which correlates with the acidic conditions commonly associated with cancerous tissues, a phenomenon linked to the Warburg effect. Additionally, the incorporation of GQDs nanoparticles within the nanocomposite had a noteworthy influence on drug release in the pH 7.4 environment when compared to the nanocomposite lacking GQDs. This controlled drug release and enhanced retention under normal conditions hold promise for mitigating the adverse effects of anticancer drugs on healthy, non-cancerous cells during their journey to cancerous tissues. This controlled release mechanism in normal environments stems from intricate interactions between the drug and the nanosystem, the expansive surface area afforded by GQDs, and the hydrogen bonding within the nanosystem. The fast and one-time release of the drugs from the GQD-containing nanocarrier in an acidic environment suggests that the nanosystem is preferable to the non-GQD nanocomposite in terms of its proper mechanism for drug release in acidic environments [42,47,50].

The cytotoxicity and anti-neoplastic impact of Aga, Aga/α-Fe2O3, Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3, and Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3 @QC against HepG2 cell line were assessed using the MTT approach, and their performance was compared with that of free drug. In order to do this, several samples were incubated with cancer cell lines for a 24 h, after which the cell viability % was computed (Fig. 6 ). The non-cancerous L929 fibroblast cell line was used in similar tests to gauge the generated materials' possible adverse effects and learn more about their biocompatibility. Furthermore, a negative control group was cultured for both cell lines to verify that they behaved normally. The two cell lines' nearly 100 % cell viability in the control group attested to their superior quality. All examined compounds, with the exception of this group, had more cytotoxic effects on cancer cells than fibroblast cells. It is evident from Fig. 6 that the cytotoxicity of Aga for both cell lines is somewhat enhanced by the cross-linking of Aga and GQDS. The final nanoparticle formulation (Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3@QC) reduced the cell viability of HepG2 cells by 54 % compared to 68 % for free QC. This outcome amply demonstrates our drug delivery system's greater anticancer efficacy over the free medication. The therapeutic efficacy of the encapsulated QC has increased in comparison to its free form because of the nanocomposite's excellent encapsulation efficiency and the double emulsion's prolonged release time frame. Regarding L929 cells, there was no discernible decline in cell viability observed in any of the synthetic samples when juxtaposed with the control group, indicating the cells' strong biocompatibility. Thus, after being incubated with QC-loaded nanoparticles for 24 h, 84 % of these cells survived. This is in line with earlier research that demonstrated positively charged nanoparticles' strong biocompatibility. Furthermore, electrostatic interaction between negatively charged cell membrane and nanoparticles can be caused by significant positive surface charge. As a result, nanoparticle absorption by cells is enhanced [51]. Less QC is produced in the neutral environment of L929 cells compared to the acidic environment of HepG2 cells, which is induced by the pH response of the Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3 nanocomposite. This result is very important since it shows that the negative effects brought on by the generated nanocarriers have decreased. Furthermore, the L929 cell viability for free QC is 14 % lower than that of nanoparticles loaded with QC, indicating the effectiveness of the drug delivery system that was established. Studies conducted in vivo indicate that earlier research on polymeric nanocarriers for QC administration demonstrated this class of nanostructures' potential to increase therapeutic efficacy [52]. Compared to the control group treated with free QC (25 %), the group treated with the novel formulation had a significantly larger percentage of tumor size decrease (62 %). Aga/GQDS/α-Fe2O3 @QC will be the subject of in vivo studies in the future prior to any possible biomedical uses.

Innovative materials are reshaping the landscape of cancer treatment, offering novel solutions for delivering drugs effectively. Aga and GQDs, distinguished for their unique attributes in cancer therapy, introduce a pH-responsive strategy for delivering the potent anticancer agent, QC. Using W/O/W emulsification, we have effectively created a hydrogel nanocomposite Aga/GQDs/α-Fe2O3@QC. Extensive characterizations comprising FTIR and XRD analyses unveiled the complex arrangement, interrelationships, and makeup of the nanocarrier components. In the Aga/GQDs setup, loading and encapsulation efficiencies achieved 35 % and 67 %, respectively. With the incorporation of α-Fe2O3, substantial improvements were realized, reaching loading and encapsulation efficiencies of 47 % and 86.25 %, surpassing previous benchmarks. Furthermore, the QC release kinetics showed that the tumor microenvironment was preferred over normal physiological conditions. The remarkable effectiveness of our nanoemulsion in specifically targeting the HepG2 cancer cell type was demonstrated by MTT testing. Although biocompatibility testing was not done in this study, we acknowledge the importance of looking into this in more detail in the future. All things considered, our pH-responsive Aga/GQDs/α-Fe2O3 nanoemulsions with QC present a novel and exciting strategy for successful cancer treatment.

Mohammad Najafi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Investigation. Zahra Khoddam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mobina Masnavi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Mehrab Pourmadadi: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Majid Abdouss: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis.
